label: fiction
title: The Hand of M. Corbin
charge: Forging a will and offering it for probate
elements: The will is not the testator's.
The accused made it or procured its making.
He offered it for probate knowing it false.

beats.text: A celebrated graphologist, for the defence: the will is 'indubitably' the old man's hand — he has matched the loops, the pressure, the tremor of age.
beats.reveal_note: His rival said the opposite with equal grandeur. When two virtuosos read the same loops both ways, the loops are telling you nothing.

beats.text: An equally celebrated graphologist, for the prosecution: 'a patent forgery' — the tremor is painted on, the pen-lifts all wrong.
beats.reveal_note: Also a performance. The case is not decided by either maestro but by a fact no expert can argue with: the chemistry of the ink.

beats.text: An industrial chemist gives plain evidence: the will's ink contains a synthetic dye first manufactured two years after M. Corbin died.
beats.reveal_note: The dead do not buy new ink. Whatever hand drew the signature drew it years after the funeral.

beats.text: A stationer's receipt, entered without comment: a letter-copying outfit, sold to the nephew three weeks after the death.
beats.reveal_note: On its own, a purchase. Next to the ink, it is preparation — the workshop assembled for the document that followed.

beats.text: The judge: where experts stand opposed, look for the fact that no expertise can bend, and build outward from there.
beats.reveal_note: The instruction the jury needed: forget the duel of loops; date the ink.

twist: The battle of handwriting virtuosos was a draw, as it usually is. The ink settled it: a dye that did not exist until two years after the testator's death, applied with a copying outfit bought three weeks after his funeral. The devoted nephew nursed his uncle — then improved on his will.
